frameworks.name: evidence accumulation
frameworks.one_line: Internal belief about a hidden rule is built by integrating ambiguous sensory cues.

frameworks.name: normative Bayesian belief updating
frameworks.one_line: Normative model predicts how belief about the active rule should evolve.

frameworks.name: model-based neural decoding
frameworks.one_line: Source-level MEG signals decoded against a computational model of belief.

core_question: How does the cortex represent and update an internal belief about a hidden behavioral rule across the brain?
key_assumptions: Participants track a hidden rule by accumulating ambiguous cues
Internal decisions need not be tied to a specific motor effector
A normative model captures expected belief dynamics
manipulations: Unpredictable, hidden changes of the stimulus-response mapping rule
Ambiguous sensory cues across trials for rule inference
Visual orientation judgment task as the elementary sensory-motor decision
key_findings: Representations of the internal belief about the active rule were found in many cortical areas
Belief representations extend to occipital (visual) cortex, not only action-related frontal areas
Profiles of these representations match normative-model predictions and resemble those seen in frontal cortex during overt decisions
interpretations: A widely distributed cortical network implements evidence accumulation for internal decisions
Even visual cortex can represent high-level, abstract beliefs about task rules
